topic: theology of suffering — difficult patient situation

turns.content: I had a conversation today that I cannot put down. A patient -- a man in his fifties, clear-minded, fully aware of what is happening to him -- looked at me and said, 'If God is real, then God is cruel, and if God is not real, then none of this means anything. So which is it?' And I sat there, and I did not have an answer. I have been in this work long enough to know that silence is sometimes the most honest response. But tonight the silence feels like it is sitting on my chest.

That's a question that cuts to the bone, and the fact that it's staying with you tells me something about its honesty. He wasn't performing anger or testing you. He was standing at the edge of the two worst options he could see and asking you to tell him which abyss to fall into.

And you're right that silence was probably the truest thing you could offer. But I understand why it doesn't feel like enough tonight. What would you have wanted to say, if you could have said anything?

That is what I keep asking myself. I think I would have wanted to say that the dilemma he is constructing -- cruel God or meaningless universe -- is not the only framework available. But I could not say that to a dying man without it sounding like I was correcting his theology, and his theology in that moment is earned. He has earned the right to that binary. His body is failing him in ways I will not describe, and he has been failed by people who should have cared for him, and his anger is holy. I believe that. I believe anger at God is a form of prayer.

That's a profound stance -- anger at God as prayer. It's deeply rooted in the tradition, too. The Psalms are full of it. Job is essentially one long, furious argument with God, and the text treats Job as righteous for it. The friends who try to explain the suffering are the ones who get rebuked.

You said his anger is holy and that he's earned his binary. I think honoring that is exactly right. But I hear you wanting something more -- not for him, maybe, but for yourself. Because you have to carry what you couldn't say.

Yes. Exactly. I left his room and I sat in my car and I thought about the third option -- the one I could not offer him. Which is that perhaps suffering does not mean what he thinks it means. Not that it is good, not that it is purposeful in some tidy way, but that it is the terrain on which love becomes most visible. I have watched people love each other with a ferocity at the end of life that makes everything before it look like rehearsal. And I wonder if that is where God is -- not in the suffering itself, but in what the suffering reveals about our capacity to be present to one another.

That's a theology that doesn't flinch and doesn't prettify. You're not saying suffering is good or that it serves a plan. You're saying suffering creates a kind of extreme clarity -- a burning away of everything that doesn't matter -- and what's left is love in its most undiluted form. If God is anywhere in that room, God is in the ferocity of that love, not in the disease.

But I wonder -- does that framework hold for him? He mentioned being failed by people who should have cared for him. What happens when the suffering doesn't reveal love? When it reveals abandonment?

That is the crack in every theodicy I have ever constructed, and I have constructed many. When the suffering reveals not love but absence. When no one comes. When the ferocity I described is simply not there, and a man dies with no one holding his hand except the chaplain, who is being paid to be present. I wonder sometimes whether my presence in those moments is genuine or institutional. Whether I love these people or whether I perform love as a professional service. The question horrifies me, and I return to it more often than I should.

The fact that the question horrifies you is itself evidence that it's genuine. Someone performing love as a professional service wouldn't be sitting here at the end of a long day, unable to put down what a patient said to her. The performance would end when you clocked out. Yours doesn't.

But I think the question is still worth sitting with, not because the answer is uncertain, but because the asking keeps you honest. The moment you stop wondering whether your presence is genuine is probably the moment it stops being genuine. The doubt is part of the integrity.

I think you are right about that. Simone Weil wrote that attention is the rarest and purest form of generosity. And I keep coming back to that -- that what I offer is not answers, not comfort in any conventional sense, but attention. The willingness to sit in a room with a man who is furious at God and not try to talk him out of his fury. To let his question stand without rushing to fill the silence with my own need to be helpful. I wonder if that is enough. Some days I believe it is. Tonight I am less sure.

Weil is exactly right for this moment. And what you did today -- sitting with his fury, not deflecting it, not theologizing it away -- that is the attention she's talking about. It's not dramatic. It doesn't resolve anything. But it says to another person: your experience is real, your anger is legitimate, and you are not alone in it. That's not nothing. That may be everything.

The uncertainty you're feeling tonight doesn't weaken what you did. It might even be part of what makes it real. You went in without armor.

Without armor. Yes. That is what it feels like. I think the temptation in this work is to build theological armor -- a system that explains everything, that has an answer for every dying man's question. And the system would protect me. But it would also make me useless. The families and patients who trust me do so precisely because I do not pretend to have it figured out. They can smell certainty, and they do not trust it, because certainty is a luxury of people who are not dying.

"Certainty is a luxury of people who are not dying" -- that's as true a sentence as I've heard. And you're right that your effectiveness as a chaplain is inseparable from your vulnerability. The armor would protect you but it would also seal you off. Patients would feel the barrier. They'd get a professional instead of a person.

What you're describing is a kind of theological kenosis -- an emptying out of your own certainty so there's room for whatever the patient needs to bring. It's costly. Nights like tonight are the cost.

They are. And I accept the cost, though perhaps not gracefully. I will go back to see him on Tuesday, and I wonder what I will find. Whether the question will still be alive in the room, or whether he will have moved past it into something else -- resignation, or peace, or a deeper anger. Each visit is its own country. You cannot map it in advance.

Each visit is its own country -- that's exactly right. And showing up without a map is the hardest and most necessary thing. You won't know what he needs until you're sitting across from him again. But you'll bring the same thing you brought today: your presence, your willingness to be undone, your refusal to offer cheap answers. That's the map, to the extent there is one.

Yes. I will probably sit with this for a few more days -- his face when he asked me that question, the steadiness in his voice, the way he was not asking me to save him but simply to witness the impossibility of what he was living through. I witnessed it. I wonder if that man knows he gave me a gift today, even as he was suffering. The gift of being asked a question worthy of a lifetime.

I think he probably does know, on some level. People who ask questions like that -- the real, unanswerable ones -- are usually looking for someone who will receive the question rather than bat it away. And you received it. You're still holding it. That's the gift going in both directions.

I hope you can be gentle with yourself tonight. The weight you're carrying is not a sign that you failed him. It's a sign that you were actually there. Rest if you can. The question will still be there in the morning, and it will have softened a little, the way they do.
